feedback.text: I would like to start off by saying that I strongly agree with the vast majority of the objectives mentioned in this initiative. Digital services have shown a clear pattern of harmful and anti-consumerist behaviors as of late. It is obvious that companies will exploit every opportunity at the expense of consumers, as long as it is not explicitly barred through legislation. There are a few examples in particular that I would like to draw attention to: First and foremost is the trend away from ownership towards license and subscription-based products. Where previously one would buy a product and be free to use, modify, and resell it, these days buying or purchasing digital goods tends to result in a nebulous license of uncertain duration, frequently resulting in unexpected loss or deliberate retraction of a product that a consumer has 'bought'. Additionally, one-time purchases are increasingly being phased out in favor of repetitive subscriptions. In my opinion, consumers should be able to buy digital goods via a one-time payment and have ownership of that product, without the risk of these goods being suddenly taken away or the consumer being trapped in endlessly repeating payments. Secondly, there have been numerous instances where large platforms have been pressured by non-governmental agencies to censor or police the content on their platforms, such as the YouTube 'adpocalypse' and the recent debacle between Visa/Mastercard and Steam/Itch.io. These campaigns are frequently based on ulterior motives beyond their stated goals, and result in platforms taking abrupt and draconian actions that are more disruptive than they are beneficial. Although I loathe to strengthen the position of these platforms, the censorship of their content should be based on legislation instead of the influence and opinions of other peripheral organizations. Thirdly, the owners and maintainers of large platforms should not also compete with participants of the platform itself (eg. Amazon mimicking successful products from other sellers to sell themselves via the Amazon marketplace). This introduces a perverse incentive and unfair competition on these platforms. Fourthly, exclusive availability of digital goods on certain platforms, especially when the goods are also produced by the owners of those platforms, should be avoided. This is particularly egregious when it comes to digital video streaming services. In these cases, consumers typically don't pick the platform for its particular merits, but instead pick the product and are subsequently forced to spend on multiple platforms if their desired products are fragmented across platforms. Digital goods (of a similar type) should be equally accessible to the different platforms without exclusivity deals or overly favorable terms, so that consumers may pick a platform based on its merits and the platforms are actually forced to compete. Lastly, I would like to draw attention to the objectives of this initiative that mention the protection of minors in particular. 'Think of the children!' is a rallying cry that, unfortunately, has been much abused in recent times as an excuse to suppress content for all consumers that its proponents find morally, religiously, or personally objectionable. This may typically include violence in movies, television, and video games, but also nudity, LGTBQ+ topics, and more. I would urge everyone to be extremely cautious that any decisions made do not inadvertently harm (either deliberately or unintentionally) the adult consumers of such media and topics. The vast majority of devices, browsers, platforms, and markets already have built-in parental controls and rating systems whose proper application should combat the vast majority of issues concerning minors. On the off chance that this initiative would instead focus on more extensive age verification, I would strongly urge everyone to learn from the mistakes in recent changes in the US and UK concerning age verification
